speaker: interviewer
text: Married, Amato. And the interviewer, Rebecca [? Zeman, ?] born Carney. This is tape four. Madame Lucia--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you said you wanted to tell me more about when-- when you left Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: On your way to Italy--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what happened with, you said something about the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when the Russians-- when we arrived in Vienna, they would stop the trains. They take out-- out. We came down. And they took us prisoner again, the-- the Russians. So from May till September, we were with the Russians in-- in Vienna. We had the camp there, and they say you must work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they used to take us to the hospital, to clean the hospital. And we were sick. And we said, we are the Jewish people. We came from the concentration camp. They didn't care much, you see? And after, all of a sudden, the Italians, the Christians, the Italians, they were prisoners, they were leaving. So we left together with them, and we arrived in Milan. And then we start looking for families, you see? And for 10 days we were there. They had a very big block, where the-- the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] arriving-- what you call-- the-- the prisoners. So we were there. And after, they said, where you want to go? I say, I want to go to Rome, where I had my sister there, and my brother. They told me that he was there. He arrived safely from-- from [? Buchen ?] but I don't know from which camp. And we met him, and we cry, and we smile, you know, all the thing like this. So you were very excited--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --when you saw your whole family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and then after we saw my sister, to go-- where she used to live, it was 70 kilometers from Rome. So at that time, they had no buses. There were in little communities, you know? And we used to-- we went there and we cried. My sister-- she said her husband also, he was prisoner. He never came back yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: After we were there, he came also. And we were a very happy family there. And the Italian people from the [ITALIAN], what you call the village, they were so nice. Si, Senora, oh, you're sisters? You're sisters? I say, yes, they're two sisters and-- and my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they used to bring us, you know, that time in September is La [? Vendemmia. ?] They bring the grapes, white, black, the wine, and salamis, all the things they used to bring us. So they were so happy, these people there, when they saw us. And I was there for nearly two years in that village.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Italy?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Italy. And then we used to go to get-- to Rome, Via Condotti. Which there was a lady there, she used to help all these people from Rhodes. She became a chairman of the [NON-ENGLISH], you see? And she was in charge. So they used to give us the UNRRA, It was an American-- [ITALIAN]? Excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Parcels?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they used to give us parcel from the UNRRA. [INTERPOSING VOICES] You see, It was like Red Cross. They used to give us 3,000 liretas a month, plus food, you know, like milk and tins, meat in tins, chocolates, coffee. They used to give us very nice parcels every month.

speaker: interviewer
text: So each survivor was getting a parcel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, that's where I met Maria, your mother. She was a boyfriend, steady. She was not married that time. When I met your father, there-- because I did see him also from the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, my mom used to go and help there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it must have been quite an experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you really think when-- when you survived--

speaker: interviewee
text: Still I don't think--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you really think that that really happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't know. They used to say to me, how you survive? [INAUDIBLE] I don't know. And I was crying all the time, you know, crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have nightmares after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes, even when I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you dream about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to shout. My husband said, it's all right. Don't worry. Don't worry. I used to get up in the night, ah! You see--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you dream about?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was dreaming like the Germans, they want to catch me. And then I was running, running. And I used to shout in the night. My husband said, don't cry. It's all right. You are here. Don't worry. Don't worry. Yeah, and my children, when they saw me, they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we-- can we just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --can we just go from Italy? You stayed there two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You just took-- OK--

speaker: interviewee
text: --with my sister and these people from, I told you, from the village, they were very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just took a vacation, like a holiday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a holiday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Recovering holiday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did you decide to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I decide to do? There was an Italian fellow there, he wants me to get married. He was a farmer, and he was well off. But I didn't think to get married myself, because I saw my sister with four children struggle there in Rome, you know? And her husband was still away. And I said, I'm not getting married yet. I want to go and work. I wanted to go and work for to sell ice cream in the cafe to help, you know, like they do here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had no time for that. Because I got a letter from my husband, who's saying-- he was not my husband. He was my neighbor from Rhodes, you see? And he saw my cousin, he asked, who came from Lucy's family. And he say, they came, the three, back. So can you give me the address? So he wrote me a lovely letter saying, how you survival? He lost the mother, and father, two brothers, and sisters, you know? And so he wrote me a lovely letter. So when I replied, next time, of the letter, he said to me, if I'm not engaged to nobody, he likes my hand. He's getting-- he wants to get married with me. So he said, send me a letter. So I said to my cousin, Clara, saying, you know, Moshe say if I marry him. She said, tell him, yes. So I say, I've got no money to send a telegram. So she had some money. And I had little bit of this liretas they gave me from the UNRRA. We said, yes, I-- I accept you. Kisses from Lucy, you see. And we got engaged, you know? It was so nice, [SOBBING] my husband was. [INAUDIBLE]? I just want to get some tissue. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] OK, so that-- he-- he wrote you a letter, and you've--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --accepted to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to marry him. And then what happened next? Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Italy. Nearly a year it took us. Because they didn't let the people come into Rhodesia. It was very hard to come to Rhodesia. So I was applying all the time. And he say, if they don't let you come here, I come to Italy or there was to Portugal, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then the people, they were very nice in Italy. And they helped me. And then I came to get married, 1947, May. On May 24, I was married in the court, you know? So you went to marry in Salisbury?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Salisbury. I came to Salisbury, Rhodesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now Zimbabwe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now is Zimbabwe, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. How many children did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two children. What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Albert and Jeanette.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are they now?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son is in San Diego. He's married. They've got two children. And Jeanette, she lives with me, you see? She's not married. She works in a-- with an advertise agency. For 21 years, she's there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to Cape Town?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1978. Nearly 18 years we are here, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you came-- all your-- all your family here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or was it just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I came with myself and my husband. Because Jeanette, she used to live already in Cape Town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she was here already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my son was in Johannesburg. And they told me-- my son said, Mommy, sell the business, and you must come. And what do you do, with the two of you there alone? In the meantime, it was trouble in Rhodesia. So we decided to sell the house and the business, and we came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you actually had a lovely life in Rhodesia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were like the queens, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you only left because of the trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because the children, they went away. And we were the two of us, you know, worry. So we-- we decided to retire.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and then you came to Cape Town.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Cape Town and we had lovely time, we had, together with my husband. He was very nice husband, you know? And I miss him all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- do you remember the date that he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he died 5 of March, 1990.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, six years he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shame. So you were very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, very, very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's good. Anyway, I'm sorry about that. It's-- it's very sad but-- [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] OK. Is there anything that you would like to tell the world? Because do you know that this video is going to be put in some museums, and some university, and it's going to remain for many, many years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to give a message to the world about, you know, your experiences in the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or about what Mr. Spielberg is doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's doing very well, because when I went to see that picture, I was crying. And I went three times, four times. They say-- the people told me, how can you go and see this picture? I said, because I was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] you know? And I'm proud of him, who did this. It's-- it's really the truth. You could-- you could see other pictures from the Germany they used to show, but it was not like this one because this is the real one. Like they were telling the story and like they were killing the people. And walking, they used to shoot you for nothing, you know, and hitting you, because you're Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to see the--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why we must be proud, because we are Jewish. We suffered. Still we suffer, but God works like this, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: But people, they used to say, oh, the God-- how-- why do you suffer? Why you want to believe is God if you suffered so? I said, this is life. I say, God is God. He helps. Because to me, He help me all the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say that the people must never forget about what the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's just say so. Is it still on? Is there a message still that you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I want to say, they mustn't forget what we suffered. It's true, and we mustn't forget. Even our children and their children, they must know it was the Holocaust, you know? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I knew the mother. I knew the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Hi, Jeanette.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, Rebecca.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm introducing Jeanette, who is Madame Lucia Amato's daughter. Jeanette, would you like to say a little bit about yourself, what you do, where you work, how old you are, a little bit about your upbringing, and where you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I was born in Zimbabwe, in Salisbury, in 1950. And my parents are Lucia, and Moshe Amato. My mom is a survivor from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: I believe you've got a brother, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my brother is two years older than me. He lives in San Diego, is married, and has two children. He married a Johannesburg girl, Jacqueline [? Siegel. ?] And they have two daughters. Nicole, who's 23, and Gina who will be 21, later this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they all in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're in America. They've been there since 1979. They immigrated. I did my schooling in Salisbury, well, junior school was done in a place called, [INAUDIBLE] and then went to high school in Salisbury. And my brother was at boarding school in [INAUDIBLE] and then also did high school in Salisbury. He left Salisbury in 1969 to live in Johannesburg. He studied there, and then got into business at a clothing company, and is still in business in San Diego, also in the clothing trade. I left Salisbury in 1975, beginning of 1975, to come here on holiday. And I went for an interview for fun with an advertising agency, because the weather had turned foul, and I'm still working for them. They're an international company called, Bates. And I'm a planner and buyer, a media planner and buyer for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you enjoy it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I've been there a long time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I believe you had a very happy upbringing, and very happy family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember always when you used to come on holiday with your parents, [? I mean ?] it was your mom and dad, amore mia. [LAUGHTER] Could you-- did you have a Jewish upbringing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mom has never tried to hide her identity of being Jewish, even though she suffered?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in fact, she used to always talk very openly about being in the camps and everything. When we were small, we used to ask her what her number was. She'd tell us a telephone number, but when we were a bit older, she explained to us. And in fact, all my friends that used to come to the house, whether they were Jewish, or not Jewish, and if they had heard that she was a survivor, or had been in the camps, they were very interested, and always wanted to know what happened. And tell us the stories. And she'd carry on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she actually told you lots of stories since you were little?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, always. She was never closed about it. And still to this day, if there's ever a movie on the war, or to do with the camps, or Germans, or whatever, she always watches. She went to see I think, four or five times, watched it when it came back on TV, you know. So she's always been very open about it, has never hidden anything from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, my dad, too, actually.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Some of them, they--

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were really closed about it, and they don't want to talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a little mining town. There weren't many Jewish families. But we still learned all the Jewish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Customs--

speaker: interviewee
text: --customs and things. And yeah, I suppose we were brought up, we were very spoiled I suppose because of the hardships, you know, she'd gone through and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Overprotective.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very overprotective, still to this day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is always worried. [LAUGHTER] Worries about my brother, even though he's so many miles away, you know, the children, and his wife. Are they all right? And you know, so, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Papa [INAUDIBLE] worried, didn't he?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Yeah, ?] [? no, ?] Dad was.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very sad when your dad passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think it was the day after your daughter's bat mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: After, yeah. I heard about it. [? Sean-- ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I told him, Sean. That night I say we were happy and in the morning he's dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Sean-- ?] Yeah, he was a good man.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't think I was brought up any different, as I say, except maybe being a little bit--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't feel different to the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I suppose I'm proud that she's a survivor, and has managed to get this far, you know? And sort of, I don't think if I was in her shoes, I would have been able to survive. I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you then think maybe that's why you're a go and getter, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, I suppose. Well, my brother's more of a go-getter, I suppose, than I am. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're not doing too badly. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Your mom was saying that in the camps, there were a lot of songs that you made up--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the other girls, together, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: With the other prisoner girls. Would you like to sing a song, Madame Lucia? You-- you-- you spoke about [? Ativa ?] was made up of words--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --which-- that-- you all surviving girls learned in the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to sing the song?

speaker: interviewee
text: We used to sing that [NON-ENGLISH], you know, in the-- the song, what you call the sound.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we made a few-- few words, you know, in-- in Italian. So we-- we used to sing, [SINGING IN ITALIAN]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we used to sing and cry all the time, you know? And then before that one, we made another one when we used to sleep, you know, in the concentration camp, that [NON-ENGLISH]. We used to sing, [SINGING IN ITALIAN] [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the song we used to sing and cry, you know? Ah, yeah. Do you still mix with a lot of your friends from the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp? You know, since I came here, I told my friends, sometimes we gather together. We have tea. And now these days, you know, one lost the husband, one lost the other. So we don't see much all the time. When we see each others, we happy together.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you're still very close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I believe you call yourselves, sisters and brothers from the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah, Mrs. [? Finns ?] always says so. We are violet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you belong still to some clubs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The [NON-ENGLISH]. We are members, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jeanette, would you like to leave a message for the rest of the world? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, I-- I think it's really important that nobody forgets, and I think this is really a wonderful idea, you know, that has come to pass. And-- and it'll be really wonderful for generations to come, that they can actually look back, and hear the stories from their grandparents, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. I think it's a wonderful thing that Steven Spielberg is doing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: As somebody said, it's a mitzvah.

speaker: interviewee
text: She looks after myself now, because my husband not here. My son is really far.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, you've said that.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] other day--

speaker: interviewee
text: She loves me too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] always there--

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing, is she's a bit over generous. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Every night, she say, Mommy, you all right? When she sees me, awake or a light in my bedroom. She say, what is wrong? I say, nothing. I'm just wake up. She worries for me because I-- I had trouble with my heart, you know. Since my husband died, always I cry and miss him very much. So we're lonely, the two of us, now.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think [INTERPOSING VOICES] being a survivor made her very generous towards other people. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And you know, she won't pass anyone in the street. If there's something left over, she'll take it down to the watchman, or give somebody food, if she hears somebody is not well and yeah, I just think it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it's funny. I also do that. You know, if we go into a restaurant or something, I'll actually ask, you know, to give a certain portion of it [INTERPOSING VOICES] to the first one that comes past.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? You're ?] a caretaker, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so I suppose you grow up being more sensitive, you know, to everything around you--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, being brought up by a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though some people say it never happened, you know, only we know that it did happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and that's why I think it's fantastic, this project. It will never be forgotten.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, we were never brought up to hate the Germans, or anything like that, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Nor were we ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So yeah, it's one of those things that happened, and it must never happen again, you know. And I think it's our duty to try and not let it happen. But also we mustn't forget what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks very much being interviewed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're accepted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks, Rebecca.
